message:
fix(str): column-aware expandtabs + cased-run based title() semantics

Two pandas-parity corrections in StringAccessor, plus fixture updates
for the packets that were previously locked to the incorrect output.

expandtabs(tabsize)
- Previous implementation was a literal `str::replace('\t', &" ".repeat(n))`
  which pads every tab with exactly `tabsize` spaces, regardless of column
  position. pandas (and Python's `str.expandtabs`) pads to the *next* tab
  stop — i.e. `tabsize - (column % tabsize)` spaces — and resets the
  column on newline. This matters for any input with non-tab prefix:
    "a\tb".expandtabs(4) → "a   b"   (3 pad, not 4)
    "a\tb\tc".expandtabs(4) → "a   b   c"
    "ab\tcd\nxy\tz".expandtabs(4) → "ab  cd\nxy  z"  (col resets after \n)
- Rewritten as an explicit char loop tracking a `column` counter: `\t`
  emits `tabsize - column % tabsize` spaces (or 0 when tabsize == 0,
  matching pandas), `\n`/`\r` resets column to 0, other chars bump
  column by one.
- Fixture updates for FP-P2D-250 (basic) and FP-P2D-298 (null-hardened)
  swap the old padded outputs ("a    b", "hello    world") for the
  column-aware outputs ("a   b", "hello   world"). Both packets are
  still strict-mode.

title()
- Previous implementation treated `whitespace | '_' | '-'` as the word
  boundary and everything else as "a word char" — which titlecased
  digits ("9lives" → "9lives" instead of "9Lives") and did not reset
  after apostrophes / periods ("rock'n'roll" → "Rock'N'Roll" expected
  but old code gave "Rock'n'roll").
- Pandas uses CPython's definition: a "word" is a maximal run of cased
  characters (is_lowercase || is_uppercase). Anything non-cased (digits,
  punctuation, whitespace) acts as a boundary. The first cased char
  after a boundary is upper; subsequent cased chars in the same run
  are lower.
- Rewritten using a `previous_was_cased` flag so the boundary logic
  depends only on the cased-ness of the preceding char, not on a
  manually enumerated punctuation set.
- Added a Rust-level unit test `str_title_resets_after_punctuation_and_digits`
  covering the three canonical cases (apostrophe, period, leading
  digit) so future regressions in this area fail inside the same crate
  before a conformance fixture would notice.
- The str_expandtabs Rust-level test is extended with the cross-tab
  and multi-line cases so the column-aware column counter is
  exercised end-to-end.
